choices.text: \(\operatorname{Li}_{k_1,\ldots,k_r}(x_1,\ldots,x_r;a)\equiv (-1)^{k_1+\cdots+k_r-1}(x_1\cdots x_r)^{-1}\,\operatorname{Li}_{k_r,\ldots,k_1}(x_r^{-1},\ldots,x_1^{-1};1-a)\pmod{\text{products}}\).

choices.label: E
choices.text: \(\operatorname{Li}_{k_1,\ldots,k_r}(x_1,\ldots,x_r;a)=(-1)^{k_1+\cdots+k_r-1}(x_1\cdots x_r)\,\operatorname{Li}_{k_r,\ldots,k_1}(x_r^{-1},\ldots,x_1^{-1};1-a)\), so no lower-depth product correction terms occur at all.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

theorem:
\label{maintheore}
Let $U \subset \mathbb{R}^d$ be a (possibly unbounded) open set with $d \geq 2$. Assume that $\mathbf{G} \in L^2_{loc}(U, \mathbb{R}^d)$ and $c \in L^1_{loc}(U)$. Let $A = (a_{ij})_{1 \leq i,j \leq d}$ be a symmetric matrix of functions such that $a_{ij} \in VMO_{loc}(U)$ for all $1 \leq i,j \leq d$, where $VMO_{loc}(U)$ is defined as follows.

\begin{defn} \label{defnvmo}
Let $\omega$ be a positive continuous function on $[0,\infty)$ with $\omega(0) = 0$. The space $VMO_\omega$ consists of all functions $g \in L^1_{loc}(\mathbb{R}^d)$ such that
\[
\sup_{z \in \mathbb{R}^d,\, r < R} \, r^{-2d} \int_{B_r(z)} \int_{B_r(z)} |g(x) - g(y)| \, dx \, dy \leq \omega(R) \quad \text{for all } R \in (0,\infty).
\]
Let $B$ be an open ball in $\mathbb{R}^d$. We define $VMO(B)$ as the set of all functions $f \in L^1(B)$ for which there exists an extension $\tilde{f} \in VMO_{\omega}$  (for some modulus $\omega$) such that $\tilde{f}|_B = f$. For an open set $U \subset \mathbb{R}^d$, the space $VMO_{loc}(U)$ is defined as the collection of all functions $f \in L^1_{loc}(U)$ such that $f \in VMO(B)$ for every open ball $B \subset \mathbb{R}^d$ with $\overline{B} \subset U$.
\end{defn}

Assume moreover that ${\rm div} A \in L^2_{loc}(U, \mathbb{R}^d)$, where the meaning of ${\rm div}$ for a matrix field is as follows.

\begin{defn} \label{defndivmat}
Let $U$ be an open subset of $\mathbb{R}^d$, $\mathbf{E} = (e_1, \dots, e_d) \in L^1_{loc}(U, \mathbb{R}^d)$ and $B = (b_{ij})_{1 \leq i,j \leq d}$ be a (possibly non-symmetric) matrix of functions with $b_{ij} \in L^1_{loc}(U)$ for all $1 \leq i,j \leq d$. Let $\mathbf{F} \in L^1_{loc}(U, \mathbb{R}^d)$ and $f \in L^1_{loc}(U)$.
\begin{itemize}
\item[(i)] We say that ${\rm div} B = \mathbf{E}$ if
\[
\int_U \sum_{i,j=1}^d b_{ij} \, \partial_i \phi_j \, dx = - \int_U \sum_{j=1}^d e_j \, \phi_j \, dx \quad \text{for all } (\phi_1, \dots, \phi_d) \in C_0^\infty(U)^d.
\]
\item[(ii)] We say that ${\rm div} \mathbf{F} = f$ if
\[
\int_U \langle \mathbf{F}, \nabla \varphi \rangle \, dx = - \int_U f \varphi \, dx \quad \text{for all } \varphi \in C_0^\infty(U).
\]
\end{itemize}
\end{defn}

Also assume that $A$ is locally uniformly strictly elliptic and bounded on $U$, i.e. for every open ball $V$ with $\overline{V} \subset U$, there exist constants $\lambda_V, M_V > 0$ such that
\begin{equation} \label{locellst}
\lambda_V \| \xi \|^2 \leq \langle A(x) \xi, \xi \rangle, \quad \;\; \max_{1 \leq i,j \leq d} |a_{ij}(x)| \leq M_V
\quad \text{for a.e. } x \in V \text{ and all } \xi \in \mathbb{R}^d.
\end{equation}
Let $\tilde{\mathbf{F}} \in L^2_{loc}(U, \mathbb{R}^d)$ and $\tilde{f} \in L^1_{loc}(U)$. Suppose that $h \in L^{\infty}_{loc}(U)$ satisfies
\[
\int_U \left({\rm trace}(A \nabla^2 \varphi) + \langle \mathbf{G}, \nabla \varphi \rangle + c \varphi \right) h \, dx 
= \int_U \tilde{f} \varphi \, dx + \int_U \langle \tilde{\mathbf{F}}, \nabla \varphi \rangle \, dx \quad \text{for all } \varphi \in C_0^{\infty}(U).
\]
Then, in establishing the main theorem, $h \in H^{1,2}_{loc}(U).
sketch: To prove the main theorem, the paper’s stated strategy is to (i) “construct a constant $\lambda_0>0$ and a sub-Markovian resolvent $(R_\alpha)_{\alpha\ge \lambda_0}$ associated with the principal elliptic operator $L^A={\rm trace}(A\nabla^2)$”, and then (ii) “show that the function $h$ can be represented as the local weak $H^{1,2}$-limit of the family $(\alpha R_\alpha h)_{\alpha\ge \lambda_0}$ as $\alpha\to\infty$.” The introduction emphasizes that the proof relies on “resolvent estimates under the weaker assumptions that the coefficients belong to $VMO_{loc}(\mathbb{R}^d)$ and that ${\rm div}A\in L^2_{loc}(\mathbb{R}^d,\mathbb{R}^d)$, which enable us to derive the main result.”
question:
Let U \subset \mathbb{R}^d be an open set, possibly unbounded, with d \ge 2. Let \mathbf{G} \in L^2_{loc}(U,\mathbb{R}^d) and c \in L^1_{loc}(U). Let A=(a_{ij})_{1\le i,j\le d} be a symmetric matrix field such that each coefficient a_{ij} belongs to VMO_{loc}(U), meaning it has vanishing mean oscillation on every ball whose closure is contained in U. Assume also that the distributional divergence of A belongs to L^2_{loc}(U,\mathbb{R}^d), where \operatorname{div}A=\mathbf E means
\[
\int_U \sum_{i,j=1}^d a_{ij}\,\partial_i \psi_j\,dx=-\int_U \sum_{j=1}^d E_j\,\psi_j\,dx
\qquad \text{for all } (\psi_1,\dots,\psi_d)\in C_c^\infty(U)^d.
\]
Assume further that A is locally uniformly strictly elliptic and locally bounded on U: for every open ball V with \overline V\subset U, there exist constants \lambda_V,M_V>0 such that
\[
\lambda_V|\xi|^2\le \langle A(x)\xi,\xi\rangle,\qquad \max_{i,j}|a_{ij}(x)|\le M_V
\]
for a.e. x\in V and all \xi\in\mathbb{R}^d. Let \tilde{\mathbf F}\in L^2_{loc}(U,\mathbb{R}^d) and \tilde f\in L^1_{loc}(U). Suppose h\in L^\infty_{loc}(U) satisfies
\[
\int_U \big(\operatorname{trace}(A\nabla^2\varphi)+\langle \mathbf G,\nabla\varphi\rangle + c\varphi\big)h\,dx
= \int_U \tilde f\,\varphi\,dx + \int_U \langle \tilde{\mathbf F},\nabla\varphi\rangle\,dx
\qquad \text{for all }\varphi\in C_c^\infty(U),
\]
where \operatorname{trace}(A\nabla^2\varphi)=\sum_{i,j=1}^d a_{ij}\,\partial_{ij}\varphi. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about every such h?

choices.label: A
choices.text: h belongs to H^{1,2}_{loc}(U).

choices.label: B
choices.text: h belongs to H^{1,2}_{loc}(U), and for every open ball V with \overline V\subset U there exists a constant C_V depending only on the local coefficients and data such that \|h\|_{H^{1,2}(V)}\le C_V\big(\|\tilde{\mathbf F}\|_{L^2(V)}+\|\tilde f\|_{L^1(V)}\big).

choices.label: C
choices.text: h belongs to L^2_{loc}(U).

choices.label: D
choices.text: h has a representative that is locally Hölder continuous on U.

choices.label: E
choices.text: h belongs to H^{2,2}_{loc}(U).

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: h belongs to H^{1,2}_{loc}(U).

theorem:
\label{theorem: intro}
	Consistently from a measurable cardinal, there are two $\sigma$-complete ultrafilters $\mathcal{V}, \mathcal{W}$ such that $\mathcal{V}$ is Lipschitz below $\mathcal{W}$, but $\mathcal{V}$ and $\mathcal{W}$ are Ketonen-incomparable. Furthermore, the same is also consistent with the assumption that the Weak Ultrapower Axiom holds.
question: Assume the consistency strength of a measurable cardinal. A \(\sigma\)-complete ultrafilter is an ultrafilter closed under countable intersections. Using the standard comparison notions \(<_L\) (the Lipschitz order) and \(<_k\) (the Ketonen order) on \(\sigma\)-complete ultrafilters, where “Ketonen-incomparable” means that neither ultrafilter is below the other in \(<_k\), and writing WUA for the Weak Ultrapower Axiom, what is the strongest statement that can be proved about consistency phenomena for \(\sigma\)-complete ultrafilters?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Relative to a measurable cardinal, it is consistent that there exist two \(\sigma\)-complete ultrafilters \(\mathcal V\) and \(\mathcal W\) such that \(\mathcal V\) is Lipschitz below \(\mathcal W\), while \(\mathcal V\) and \(\mathcal W\) are Ketonen-incomparable; moreover, it is also consistent that such a situation occurs together with the Weak Ultrapower Axiom.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Relative to a measurable cardinal, it is consistent that there exist two \(\sigma\)-complete ultrafilters \(\mathcal V\) and \(\mathcal W\) such that \(\mathcal V\) is Lipschitz below \(\mathcal W\), while \(\mathcal V\) and \(\mathcal W\) are Ketonen-incomparable; however, no model satisfying the Weak Ultrapower Axiom can contain such a pair.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Relative to a measurable cardinal, it is consistent that there exist two \(\sigma\)-complete ultrafilters \(\mathcal V\) and \(\mathcal W\) such that \(\mathcal V\) is Lipschitz below \(\mathcal W\), while \(\mathcal V\) and \(\mathcal W\) are Ketonen-incomparable.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Relative to a measurable cardinal, it is consistent that there exist two \(\sigma\)-complete ultrafilters \(\mathcal V\) and \(\mathcal W\) such that \(\mathcal V\) is Lipschitz below \(\mathcal W\) and the Lipschitz and Ketonen orders do not coincide on \(\sigma\)-complete ultrafilters; nevertheless, in every model of the Weak Ultrapower Axiom, any relation \(\mathcal V<_L\mathcal W\) already implies that \(\mathcal V\) and \(\mathcal W\) are comparable in the Ketonen order.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Relative to a measurable cardinal, it is consistent that the Lipschitz and Ketonen orders on \(\sigma\)-complete ultrafilters can fail to coincide, but only outside the Weak Ultrapower Axiom; under WUA, the Ketonen order is always linear on \(\sigma\)-complete ultrafilters.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: Relative to a measurable cardinal, it is consistent that there exist two \(\sigma\)-complete ultrafilters \(\mathcal V\) and \(\mathcal W\) such that \(\mathcal V\) is Lipschitz below \(\mathcal W\), while \(\mathcal V\) and \(\mathcal W\) are Ketonen-incomparable; moreover, it is also consistent that such a situation occurs together with the Weak Ultrapower Axiom.

theorem:
\label{t:main}
  Let $q\ge4$ be a prime power and $G$ be almost simple with socle
  $S=\PSL_2(q)$, that is $S\le G\le\aut(S)$. Suppose that $G$ acts
  primitively on $\Omega$. Then for every distinct
  $\alpha, \beta\in\Omega$ there exists $s\in S$ such that
  $\alpha^s=\beta$ and $s$ fixes no point of $\Omega$.
sketch: To prove the main theorem, one uses that when $G$ acts primitively on $\Omega$, “the possibilities for a point stabilizer $S_\omega < S$ are known.” The argument then splits by the type of $S_\omega$: “some cases are relatively easy to handle,” while the “more difficult ones arise when $S_\omega$ is dihedral, or when $S_\omega$ is one of the polyhedral groups $A_4$, $S_4$, or $A_5$.” In the dihedral case, the proof “require[s] Hasse’s bound on the number of points on elliptic curves over finite fields,” and to exclude the polyhedral cases one “derive[s] trace identities for the binary polyhedral groups $2A_4$, $2S_4$, and $2A_5$.” The authors note that “in some cases there are lengthy calculations” and they “provide SageMath code at \cite{dera_psl2_verify} that confirms the claims.”
question: Let q\ge 4 be a prime power, let G be an almost simple group with socle S=\mathrm{PSL}_2(q) (so S\le G\le \mathrm{Aut}(S)), and suppose G acts primitively on a set \Omega. Here, for g\in G and \omega\in\Omega, \omega^g denotes the image of \omega under the action. For distinct points \alpha,\beta\in\Omega, what is the strongest statement that can be proved about the existence of an element sending \alpha to \beta while fixing no point of \Omega?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: For every pair of distinct points \alpha,\beta\in\Omega, there exists an involution s\in S such that \alpha^s=\beta and s fixes no point of \Omega.

choices.label: C
choices.text: For every pair of distinct points \alpha,\beta\in\Omega, there exists an element g\in G such that \alpha^g=\beta and g fixes no point of \Omega.

choices.label: D
choices.text: For every pair of distinct points \alpha,\beta\in\Omega, there exists an element s\in S such that \alpha^s=\beta and every nontrivial power of s fixes no point of \Omega.

choices.label: E
choices.text: There exists a single conjugacy class C\subseteq S such that for every pair of distinct points \alpha,\beta\in\Omega, some element s\in C satisfies \alpha^s=\beta and s fixes no point of \Omega.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

question:
Let \(\mathcal C\) be a block-stable class of connected graphs, and let \(\mathcal B\) be the class of its 2-connected components (blocks). Write \(B(y)\) for the exponential generating function of \(\mathcal B\), let \(\rho_B\) be the radius of convergence of \(B\), and define
\[
u_C:=\frac{1}{\rho_B B''(\rho_B)}.
\]
Let \(C^{\bullet}(x,u)\) be the exponential generating function of connected rooted graphs in \(\mathcal C\), where \(x\) marks vertices and \(u\) marks blocks. For fixed \(u>0\), let \(\mathbb P_\nu\) denote the fixed-size block-weighted law under which a rooted graph \(G\in\mathcal C^{\bullet}\) with \(n\) vertices is sampled with probability proportional to \(u^{b(G)}\), where \(b(G)\) is the number of blocks of \(G\). The block tree of a sampled graph is the tree recording how its blocks are attached. Which statement holds for every such class \(\mathcal C\)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The behaviour changes at the threshold \(u_C=1/(\rho_B B''(\rho_B))\): the singular behaviour of \(C^{\bullet}(x,u)\) depends on whether \(u<u_C\) or not, and the block tree of a graph sampled according to \(\mathbb P_\nu\) has a subcritical Bienaymé–Galton–Watson law when \(u<u_C\), while it has a critical Bienaymé–Galton–Watson law when \(u\ge u_C\).

choices.label: B
choices.text: The behaviour changes at the threshold \(u_C=1/(\rho_B B''(\rho_B))\): the singular behaviour of \(C^{\bullet}(x,u)\) depends on whether \(u<u_C\) or not, and the block tree of a graph sampled according to \(\mathbb P_\nu\) has a subcritical Bienaymé–Galton–Watson law when \(u<u_C\), a critical Bienaymé–Galton–Watson law only at \(u=u_C\), and a supercritical Bienaymé–Galton–Watson law when \(u>u_C\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: The behaviour changes at the threshold \(u_C=1/(\rho_B B''(\rho_B))\): in particular, the singular behaviour of \(C^{\bullet}(x,u)\) depends on whether \(u<u_C\) or not.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The behaviour changes at the threshold \(u_C=1/(\rho_B B''(\rho_B))\): the singular behaviour of \(C^{\bullet}(x,u)\) depends on whether \(u<u_C\) or not, and under \(\mathbb P_\nu\) it is the decorated block tree itself that has a subcritical Bienaymé–Galton–Watson law when \(u<u_C\) and a critical one when \(u\ge u_C\), whereas the underlying undecorated block tree need not be Bienaymé–Galton–Watson.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The behaviour changes at the threshold \(u_C=1/(\rho_B B''(\rho_B))\): the singular behaviour of \(C^{\bullet}(x,u)\) depends on whether \(u<u_C\) or not, but after conditioning on the size \(n\) the block tree of a graph sampled according to \(\mathbb P_\nu\) always has a critical Bienaymé–Galton–Watson law, for every \(u>0\).

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: The behaviour changes at the threshold \(u_C=1/(\rho_B B''(\rho_B))\): the singular behaviour of \(C^{\bullet}(x,u)\) depends on whether \(u<u_C\) or not, and the block tree of a graph sampled according to \(\mathbb P_\nu\) has a subcritical Bienaymé–Galton–Watson law when \(u<u_C\), while it has a critical Bienaymé–Galton–Watson law when \(u\ge u_C\).

theorem:
[Gradient regularity in Morrey spaces]\label{MainThm}
Let $u \in W^{1,p}(\Omega)$ be a local minimizer of the functional $\mathcal{F}(\cdot;\Omega)$ defined in
\begin{equation}\label{MainF}
\mathcal{F}(u;E) := \int_{\Omega} \sigma_E(x)\, F(\nabla u)\, dx,
\end{equation}
and assume that $\partial E$ is a hypersurface of class $C^1$.
Suppose that assumptions \emph{(H1)}--\emph{(H2)} are satisfied where $2<p<\frac{2n}{n-2}$.
Then, for every $0 \leq \lambda < n$,
\[
\nabla u \in L^{{p},\lambda}_{\mathrm{loc}}(\Omega).
\]
Moreover, for every $\Omega' \Subset \Omega$, there exists a constant
$C = C\big(n, p, \nu, L, \beta, E, \operatorname{diam}(\Omega),
\operatorname{dist}(\Omega', \partial\Omega)\big) > 0$
such that
\[
\|\nabla u\|_{L^{{p},\lambda}(\Omega')} \leq C.
\]
sketch:
The proof of the main theorem is obtained as a consequence of the decay estimate for the energy stated in the following proposition.

\begin{Prop}\label{FJNL}
Let $u$ be a local minimizer of the functional $\mathcal{F}(\cdot;\Omega)$ defined
in \eqref{MainF} under the assumption of Theorem~\ref{MainThm}.
Then there exists a constant $0 < \tau_0 < 1$ such that the following holds:
for every $\tau \in (0,\tau_0)$ there exists $\varepsilon_0 = \varepsilon_0(\tau) > 0$
with the property that, if $B_r(x_0) \Subset \Omega$ and one of the following
conditions is satisfied,
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
\item $|E \cap B_r(x_0)| < \varepsilon_0 |B_r|$,
\item $|B_r(x_0) \setminus E| < \varepsilon_0 |B_r|$,
\item there exists a half-space $H$ such that
\[
\frac{|(E \Delta H) \cap B_r(x_0)|}{|B_r|} < \varepsilon_0,
\]
\end{enumerate}
then, for every $0 < \delta < n$, the estimate
\[
\int_{B_{\tau r}(x_0)} |\nabla u|^p \, dx
\leq
C_0 \tau^{n-\delta}
\left(
\int_{B_r(x_0)} |\nabla u|^p \, dx + r^n
\right)
\]
holds, where the constant $C_0$ depends only on
$n, \nu, L, \alpha,\beta, \delta$ and $\|\nabla u\|_{L^2(\Omega)}$.
\end{Prop}

An outline is given for the proof of Proposition~\ref{FJNL}:
\begin{itemize}
\item The argument \emph{relies on decay estimates for the gradient near the interface}, first established in a \emph{flat configuration} (interface coincides with a hyperplane), treated next.
\item Starting point: a \emph{local boundedness estimate for the tangential gradient} $\nabla' u$ (derivatives parallel to the interface). This is proved in Proposition~3.2 via a \emph{difference-quotient argument} with test functions involving only tangential increments.
\item Key ingredient: a \emph{Moser iteration} applied to $Z:=|\Delta_{i,h}u|^{m/p}$. In the estimates, the full gradient $\nabla u$ appears through (H1)--(H2) and is \emph{treated as a weight}; this is cited as the origin of the restriction $2<p<\frac{2n}{n-2}$.
\item Since the Proposition~3.2 bound has a non-optimal decay exponent and constants that may depend on $\mu$, one obtains the \emph{scale-invariant Lipschitz estimate for $\nabla' u$} (Proposition~3.4) by a \emph{rescaling argument} requiring \emph{uniformity with respect to $\mu$}. This uniformity is proved in Lemma~3.3, using boundedness of $\nabla' u$ and a \emph{Poincar\'e-type inequality} for $Z:=|\partial_i u|^{m/p}$, $i=1,\dots,n-1$, which is admissible by Theorem~3.1.
\item With the tangential Lipschitz bound, the \emph{Morrey estimate in the flat case} is proved in Theorem~3.5 by a \emph{comparison argument}.
\item The \emph{general case} then follows by a \emph{standard flattening/approximation procedure} using the $C^1$ regularity of the interface, along the lines of the quadratic theory.
\end{itemize}

Additionally, Remark~\ref{Reg} notes that for proving the main theorem it would be sufficient to have Proposition~\ref{FJNL} only in case (i), though cases (ii)--(iii) are included for completeness.
question:
Let $\Omega\subset\mathbb{R}^n$ be a bounded domain, let $E\subset\Omega$ be measurable, fix constants $0<\alpha<\beta<+\infty$, and set
\[
\sigma_E:=\beta\,\mathbbm{1}_E+\alpha\,\mathbbm{1}_{E^c},
\]
where $\mathbbm{1}_E$ denotes the characteristic function of $E$. Let $u\in W^{1,p}(\Omega)$ be a local minimizer of the functional
\[
\mathcal F(v;E)=\int_{\Omega} \sigma_E(x)\,F(\nabla v)\,dx,
\]
meaning that for every ball $B_r(x_0)\subset\Omega$ and every $\phi\in W^{1,p}_0(B_r(x_0))$ one has
\[
\mathcal F(u;B_r(x_0))\le \mathcal F(u+\phi;B_r(x_0)).
\]
Assume that $\partial E$ is a hypersurface of class $C^1$, that the structural assumptions $(H1)$--$(H2)$ hold, and that $2<p<\frac{2n}{n-2}$. Fix $0\le \lambda<n$. Here $\Omega'\Subset\Omega$ means that $\Omega'$ is compactly contained in $\Omega$, and $L^{p,\lambda}_{\mathrm{loc}}(\Omega)$ means membership in $L^{p,\lambda}(\Omega')$ for every such $\Omega'$. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the Morrey-space regularity of $\nabla u$ under these assumptions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text:
For every $\Omega'\Subset\Omega$, there exists a constant
$C=C\big(n,p,\nu,L,\beta,\operatorname{diam}(\Omega),\operatorname{dist}(\Omega',\partial\Omega)\big)>0$
such that
\[
\|\nabla u\|_{L^{p,\lambda}(\Omega')}\le C.
\]
In particular, $\nabla u\in L^{p,\lambda}_{\mathrm{loc}}(\Omega)$.

choices.label: C
choices.text:
For every $\Omega'\Subset\Omega$, there exists a constant
$C=C\big(n,p,\nu,L,\beta,E,\operatorname{diam}(\Omega),\operatorname{dist}(\Omega',\partial\Omega)\big)>0$
such that
\[
\|\nabla u\|_{L^{p,0}(\Omega')}\le C.
\]
In particular, $\nabla u\in L^{p}_{\mathrm{loc}}(\Omega)$.

choices.label: D
choices.text:
There exists a constant
$C=C\big(n,p,\nu,L,\beta,E,\operatorname{diam}(\Omega)\big)>0$
such that
\[
\|\nabla u\|_{L^{p,\lambda}(\Omega)}\le C.
\]
In particular, $\nabla u\in L^{p,\lambda}(\Omega)$.

choices.label: E
choices.text:
For every $\Omega'\Subset\Omega$, there exists a constant
$C=C\big(n,p,\nu,L,\beta,E,\operatorname{diam}(\Omega),\operatorname{dist}(\Omega',\partial\Omega)\big)>0$
such that
\[
\|\nabla u\|_{L^{q,\lambda}(\Omega')}\le C
\qquad\text{for every }q\in[p,sp],
\]
where $s>1$ is the higher-integrability exponent. In particular, $\nabla u\in L^{q,\lambda}_{\mathrm{loc}}(\Omega)$ for every $q\in[p,sp]$.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

theorem:
\label{th:BC:u}
	If the underlying probability space $(\Omega,\mathscr{F},\mathrm{P})$ is 
	complete, then:
	\begin{enumerate}[\noindent (1)]
	\item There exists a strictly decreasing, convex, 
		and continuous function $\lambda: (0\,,\infty) \to (0\,,\infty)$, 
		independent of $\sigma$, such that $\lim_{r\to0^+} \lambda(r) = \infty$, 
		$\lim_{r \to \infty} \lambda(r) = 0$, and 
		\[
			\mathrm{P}\left\{ |u(t\,,0)-1| \leqslant |\sigma(1)| \theta t^{1/4}\ \ 
			\forall t \in [\varepsilon f(\varepsilon),\varepsilon]\right\} 
			= f(\varepsilon)^{\lambda(\theta)+\mathscr{o}(1)}\quad
			\text{as $\varepsilon\to0$},
		\]
		for every function $f:(0\,,1/{\rm e})\to(0\,,1/{\rm e})$ that vanishes at zero and satisfies
		$f(\varepsilon) \geqslant \varepsilon^{\ell+\mathscr{o}(1)}$, as $\varepsilon\to0^+$,
		for a fixed number $\ell=\ell(f)>0$.
	\item Let $K$ denote a nonrandom compact set in $\mathbb{R}$. Then,
		\[
			\mathrm{P}\{\fS(\theta)\cap K\neq\varnothing\}=
			\begin{cases}
				1 & \text{if $\lambda(\theta) < \frac12\dimh K$},\\
				0 & \text{if $\lambda(\theta) > \frac12\dimmm K$},
			\end{cases}
		\]
		where $\dimmm$ and $\dimh$ respectively the lower Minkowski (or box) dimension
		and the Hausdorff dimension (see Falconer \textnormal{\cite{Falconer}}); and
	\item  There exists a single $\mathrm{P}$-null set off which $\dimh \fS(\theta) = 1 - 2\lambda(\theta)$
		for every 
		\begin{equation}\label{theta_c}
			\theta\geqslant \theta_c := \lambda^{-1}(\tfrac12).
		\end{equation}
	\end{enumerate}
sketch:
A proof sketch appears only in the discussion of measurability:

\begin{remark}\label{rem:meas}
	As usual, the completeness condition for the underlying probability space
	can be made without loss in generality. It is used here in order to overcome 
	potential measurability issues. For instance, we establish Part (2) by proving 
	that $\{\omega\in\Omega:\ \fS(\theta)\cap K\neq\varnothing\}$
	is a subset of a $\P$-null set when $\lambda(\theta) > \frac12\dimmm K$,
	and contains a full-probability set when $\lambda(\theta) < \frac12\dimh K$.
	Likewise, Part (3) is proved by showing
	that the set $\{\omega\in\Omega:\ \dimh \fS(\theta)(\omega) \neq 1 - 
	2\lambda(\theta)\}$ lies in a $\P$-null set. In the sequel, we 
	might suppress discussions
	on measurability. In all such cases, measurability issues can be verified individually using this type of
	inclusion/exclusion idea.
\end{remark}

- The completeness assumption on $(\Omega,\mathscr{F},\mathrm{P})$ is used to handle “potential measurability issues.”

- In establishing the main theorem, Part~(2) is handled by proving that $\{\omega\in\Omega:\ \fS(\theta)\cap K\neq\varnothing\}$ is a subset of a $\mathrm{P}$-null set when $\lambda(\theta) > \frac12\dimmm K$, and contains a full-probability set when $\lambda(\theta) < \frac12\dimh K$.

- In establishing the main theorem, Part~(3) is proved by showing that the set $\{\omega\in\Omega:\ \dimh \fS(\theta)(\omega) \neq 1 - 2\lambda(\theta)\}$ lies in a $\mathrm{P}$-null set.

- They indicate that later they may “suppress discussions on measurability,” since such issues “can be verified individually using this type of inclusion/exclusion idea.”

No further proof outline for the analytic content (e.g., the construction/properties of $\lambda$ in Part~(1), or the probabilistic/fractal arguments beyond the above null/full-probability inclusions) is provided in the post-theorem introduction.
question:
Let $u=u(t,x)$ be the solution to the stochastic heat equation on $(0, ty)\times\mathbb R$
\[
\partial_t u(t,x)=\partial_x^2 u(t,x)+\sigma(u(t,x))\,\dot W(t,x),\qquad u(0,x)=1\ \text{for all }x\in\mathbb R,
\]
where $\sigma:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R$ is a nonrandom Lipschitz continuous function with $\sigma(1)\neq 0$, and $\dot W$ denotes space-time white noise. Assume the underlying probability space $(\Omega,\mathscr F,\mathrm P)$ is complete. Let
\[
\left\{|u(t,0)-1|\le |\sigma(1)|\,\theta\, t^{1/4}\ \text{for all } t\in[\varepsilon f(\varepsilon),\varepsilon]\right\}
\]
be the small-time event of interest, where $f:(0,1/e)\to(0,1/e)$ satisfies $f(\varepsilon)\to0$ as $\varepsilon\to0^+$ and
\[
f(\varepsilon)\ge \varepsilon^{\ell+o(1)}\quad\text{as }\varepsilon\to0^+
\]
for some fixed number $\ell=\ell(f)>0$. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the asymptotic probability of this event as $\varepsilon\to0^+$?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text:
There exists a strictly decreasing, convex, and continuous function \(\lambda:(0,\infty)\to(0,\infty)\), independent of \(\sigma\), such that \(\lim_{r\to0^+}\lambda(r)=\infty\), \(\lim_{r\to\infty}\lambda(r)=0\), and for every such \(f\) and every \(\theta>0\),
\[
\lim_{\varepsilon\to0^+}\frac{\log \mathrm P\left\{|u(t,0)-1|\le |\sigma(1)|\,\theta\, t^{1/4}\ \text{for all }t\in[\varepsilon f(\varepsilon),\varepsilon]\right\}}{\log \varepsilon}
=\lambda(\theta).
\]

choices.label: C
choices.text:
There exists a function \(\lambda:(0,\infty)\to(0,\infty)\), independent of \(\sigma\), such that for every such \(f\) and every \(\theta>0\),
\[
\mathrm P\left\{|u(t,0)-1|\le |\sigma(1)|\,\theta\, t^{1/4}\ \text{for all }t\in[\varepsilon f(\varepsilon),\varepsilon]\right\}
=f(\varepsilon)^{\lambda(\theta)+o(1)}\qquad\text{as }\varepsilon\to0^+.
\]

choices.label: D
E
choices.text:
There exists a strictly decreasing, convex, and continuous function \(\lambda:(0,\infty)\to(0,\infty)\), independent of \(\sigma\), such that \(\lim_{r\to0^+}\lambda(r)=\infty\), \(\lim_{r\to\infty}\lambda(r)=0\), and for every such \(f\) and every \(\theta>0\),
\[
\mathrm P\left\{|u(t,0)-1|\le |\sigma(1)|\,\theta\, t^{1/4}\ \text{for all }t\in[0,\varepsilon]\right\}
=f(\varepsilon)^{\lambda(\theta)+o(1)}\qquad\text{as }\varepsilon\to0^+.
\]

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

theorem:
\label{thm:main}
Let $n(B)$ be the number of triples $u,v,w\in\mathbb{N}$ of square-full integers, such that $u+v=w$ and
$w\le B$. Then
\[n(B)\ll_\varepsilon B^{3/5-3/1555+\varepsilon}\]
for any fixed $\varepsilon>0$.
sketch:
To see how the main theorem follows from Theorem \ref{thm2} one argues as follows. Given $u+v=w$ with positive square-full integers, let $h=\gcd(u,v,w)$. Then “$h$ must be square-full”, and writing square-full integers as $x^2y^3$ with $y$ square-free yields decompositions $u=ha_1x_1^2y_1^3$ with $a_1\mid h$, $(a_1,x_1y_1)=1$, and $a_1y_1$ square-free (and similarly for $v,w$), leading to the bound
\[
 n(B)\le\sum_{\substack{h\le B\\ h\ \mathrm{square\text{-}full}}}\ \sum_{a_1,a_2,a_3\mid h} n(B/h;a_1,a_2,a_3).
\]
We first use the following theorem.
\begin{theorem}\label{thm2}
Let $a_1,a_2,a_3$ be non-zero square-free integers (of either sign) which are coprime in
pairs. Let $n(B;a_1,a_2,a_3)$ be the number of positive integer solutions
$x_1,x_2,x_3,y_1,y_2,y_3$ of the equation
\beql{axy}
a_1x_1^2y_1^3+a_2x_2^2y_2^3+a_3x_3^2y_3^3=0
\eeq
with $a_1y_1,a_2y_2$ and $a_3y_3$ square-free, and for which
\beql{range}
|a_1|x_1^2y_1^3,|a_2|x_2^2y_2^3,|a_3|x_3^2y_3^3\le B
\eeq
and
\beql{gcd}
\gcd(x_1y_1\,,\,x_2y_2\,,\,x_3y_3)=\gcd(a_1a_2a_3\,,\,x_1x_2x_3y_1y_2y_3)=1.
\eeq
Then $n(B;a_1,a_2,a_3)\ll_\ep B^{3/5-3/1555+\ep}$, for any fixed $\ep>0$.
\end{theorem}
Applying the theorem above gives
\[
 n(B)\ll_\varepsilon\sum_{\substack{h\le B\\ h\ \mathrm{square\text{-}full}}}\left(\frac{B}{h}\right)^{3/5-3/1555+\varepsilon}\tau(h)^3
\ll_\varepsilon B^{3/5-3/1555+\varepsilon},
\]
using that $a_1,a_2,a_3$ may be of either sign and that if $h$ runs over square-full integers then $\sum_h h^{-\theta}<\infty$ for $\theta>\tfrac12$.

For Theorem \ref{thm2}, “the main element in our strategy” is to study the bi-homogeneous congruence
\[
a_1x_1^2y_1^3+a_2x_2^2y_2^3\equiv 0\ (\mathrm{mod}\ y_3^3).
\]
After “various manoeuvres” one shows $(x_1,x_2,y_1,y_2)$ lies on “one of a small number of lines in $\mathbb{P}^3(\mathbb{Q})$ or, more precisely, on 2-dimensional lattices $\mathsf{M}\subset\mathbb{Z}^4$.” These lead to equations
\[
F(u,v)=-a_3y_3^3\square,
\]
with $F$ a degree-5 form, which are treated by the “square-sieve”. A key input is control of “the size of $\det(\mathsf{M})$ for these lattices,” which governs the range of $u,v$, and “various special cases” are handled separately. An auxiliary ingredient highlighted is Theorem \ref{cubic}, bounding primitive solutions to $ax^3+by^3+cz^3=0$ by $\rho(B;a,b,c)\ll_\varepsilon B^\varepsilon$, with the note that in this particular situation the ranks of the associated elliptic curves can be estimated “quite efficiently.”
question: A positive integer is called square-full if every prime dividing it divides it to exponent at least 2. For a real parameter $B\ge 1$, let $n(B)$ denote the number of triples $(u,v,w)$ of positive integers, each square-full, satisfying $u+v=w$ and $w\le B$. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the quantitative estimate for $n(B)$?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text:
For every fixed $\varepsilon>0$ one has
\[
n(B)\ll_{\varepsilon} B^{3/5-1/500+\varepsilon}.
\]

choices.label: C
choices.text:
There exists a constant $\delta>0$ such that
\[
n(B)\ll B^{3/5-\delta}.
\]

choices.label: D
choices.text:
For every fixed $\varepsilon>0$ one has
\[
n(B)\ll_{\varepsilon} B^{1/2+\varepsilon}.
\]

choices.label: E
choices.text:
For every fixed $\varepsilon>0$ one has
\[
n(B)\ll B^{3/5-3/1555+\varepsilon},
\]
with an implied constant absolute (in particular, independent of $\varepsilon$).

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

theorem:
\label{thm:main}
Let $F$ be the complete flag variety of vector subspaces of $\C^4$, isotropic with respect to a nondegenerate skew-symmetric form, endowed with the natural action of the complex torus $H\subset\PSP(4)$ of homothety classes of diagonal matrices. Then the normalized Chow quotient $X$ of $F$ by the action of $H$ is a singular del Pezzo surface of degree $4$ with two singularities of type $A_1$.
sketch: We "follow the lines of argumentation of \cite{BOS2}, adapted to our particular situation." After preliminaries on torus actions and on the complete flag $F$ of type $\DC_2$, the authors "recall some known facts on its fixed points by the action of the maximal torus $H$, and introduce some particular $\C^*$-actions, that later on will play a central role in the study of the boundary divisors of the Chow quotient." Next, they study "the combinatorial quotients of $H$-invariant affine charts in $F$, which contain information about the local degeneration of the closure of the general $H$-orbit in $F$." Finally, in Section \ref{sec:mainproof} they "prove the main statement of the paper, showing how to construct the Chow quotient $X$ as an inverse limit of combinatorial quotients," and then conclude with a description of the geometry of $X$.
question:
Let $\Omega$ be a nondegenerate skew-symmetric form on $\mathbb{C}^4$, and let
$$F=\{(V_1,V_2)\in \mathrm{G}(1,4)\times \mathrm{G}(2,4)\mid V_1\subset V_2,\ \text{and }V_1,V_2\text{ are }\Omega\text{-isotropic}\}$$
be the complete flag variety of $\Omega$-isotropic lines contained in $\Omega$-isotropic planes in $\mathbb{C}^4$. Let $H\subset \mathrm{PSP}(4)$, where $\mathrm{PSP}(4)$ is the projective symplectic group of $\Omega$, be the complex torus of homothety classes of diagonal matrices acting naturally on $F$. What is the normalized Chow quotient $X$ of $F$ by $H$?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A singular del Pezzo surface of degree 4 with two singularities of type $A_1$.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A singular del Pezzo surface of degree 4 with one singularity of type $A_1$ and one singularity of type $A_2$.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A singular del Pezzo surface of degree 4.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A singular del Pezzo surface of degree 4 with two singularities of type $A_2$.

choices.label: E
choices.text: A singular del Pezzo surface of degree 5 with two singularities of type $A_1$.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: A singular del Pezzo surface of degree 4 with two singularities of type $A_1$.
